frameworks.name: serial dependence
frameworks.one_line: current orientation report biased toward previously seen stimuli

frameworks.name: post-perceptual / decisional account of serial dependence
frameworks.one_line: bias arises at decision/memory stages, not low-level visual encoding

frameworks.name: perceptual / low-level account of serial dependence
frameworks.one_line: claim that SD originates within early visual processing

core_question: Does serial dependence in orientation reports originate at low-level perceptual stages or at post-perceptual (e.g., decisional) stages?
key_assumptions: perceived similarity gates low-level perceptual carryover between trials
Gabors of different spatial frequencies and Gabor-vs-dot patterns are perceptually clearly dissimilar
robust SD across dissimilar stimuli implicates non-perceptual stages
SD is typically strongest for orientation differences below ~45° over a few past trials
manipulations: spatial frequency of successive Gabor patches
stimulus type intermixed across trials (Gabor vs. dot patterns)
orientation difference between current and previous trial
key_findings: robust serial dependence persisted across Gabors with different spatial frequencies
robust serial dependence persisted when Gabors were intermingled with dot patterns
SD effects emerged even though successive stimuli were perceptually clearly dissimilar
interpretations: serial dependence does not originate from low-level visual processing
current models and theoretical accounts of SD should be re-evaluated in favor of post-perceptual loci
